mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series 325i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 325i engine typically costs £2,000–£4,500 supply only depending on generation, with older M20/M50/M52 units lower and the N20 higher. Fitting adds £900–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 325i.

It depends which generation your 325i is. The M54 (E46) is widely considered the most reliable of the bunch — smooth, proven, with cooling system as its main weakness. The N20 (F30) is the most powerful but has timing chain guide issues. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 325i engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: E30–E46: cooling system failure (water pump, thermostat, radiator). E90 N53: Piezo injector failure and NOx sensor issues. F30 N20: timing chain guide failure. Each generation has its own character and watchpoints.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 325i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. An E30 325i is a classic — values range from £5,000–£25,000, making replacement viable. An E46 325i is a £2,000–£8,000 vehicle — a £2,500–£3,000 recon is 38–150% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples (sub‑£4,000), replacement may exceed our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 325i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 325i covers M20 (E30), M50/M52 (E36), M54 (E46), N52/N53 (E90), and N20 (F30). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a 325i?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M20–M54 engines: regular cooling system maintenance is the priority — replace water pump, thermostat and expansion tank every 60,000 miles. On N53: use high‑quality fuel and have injectors tested periodically. On N20: regular oil changes with BMW Longlife‑01 FE spec oil and shortened intervals are essential. At the first sign of rattle or overheating, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide Failure (N20, F30 2012–2015)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power, chain rattle on acceleration.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (chain guide and tensioner replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before chain snaps)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N20's timing chain guide is the defining weakness of this engine — a well‑documented issue that can lead to catastrophic engine failure. BMW issued a recall for this, but many cars are now out of warranty. If you hear a rattling noise on cold start, have the chain inspected immediately. Regular oil changes with BMW Longlife‑01 FE spec oil are essential.

problems.title: Cooling System Failure (M20, M50, M52, M54 — E30, E36, E46)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature gauge fluctuations, steam from the engine bay, heater not working.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (water pump, thermostat, radiator) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: Across all pre‑E90 325i generations, the cooling system is the defining weakness — plastic thermostat housings and water pump impellers fail with age. Replace the water pump and thermostat housing preventatively every 60,000 miles. If you notice temperature fluctuations, pull over immediately — overheating can kill these engines quickly.

problems.title: Injector / NOx Sensor Issues (N53, E90 2007–2013)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfire, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, failed emissions test.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,500 (injector set replacement) / £200–£400 (NOx sensor) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.recommendation: The N53's direct‑injection system uses Piezo injectors that can fail, and NOx sensors are also a known weakness. If you experience rough running or a persistent engine warning light, have the injectors tested and NOx sensor checked. Replacing injectors as a set is recommended.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 325i? Check These First
buyIf: * N20 (F30): timing chain replaced or inspected (or proof of recall work)
* M54 (E46): cooling system replaced (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank)
* N53 (E90): injectors replaced with latest revision
* Full service history with BMW-spec oil for the correct era
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle on N20 — timing chain guides may be failing — walk away
* Temperature fluctuations on E30/E36/E46 — cooling system may be compromised
* Rough idle on N53 — injectors may be failing
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 325i badge is a journey through BMW straight‑six history — from the classic M20 to the modern N20 turbo. The M54 is widely considered the sweet spot for reliability; the N20 is the most powerful but also the most maintenance‑sensitive. Buy on generation‑specific history — and never ignore a cold‑start rattle or temperature fluctuation.

cta.label: → Compare 325i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 3 Series 325i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling; timing chain indicator (N20) / belt condition (M20)
* Temperature Gauge — Check for fluctuations; cooling system weakness (E30–E46)
* Service History — including which generation (E30/E36/E46/E90/F30) and confirmed maintenance
* CCV (M54) — Check for oil consumption or smoke; CCV may be failing
* Injector Health (N53) — Check for rough idle; injector failure indicator
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
